coredata.dc:title: Graphene oxide/graphene quantum dots: A platform for probing ds-DNA-dimethoate interaction and dimethoate sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Elshafey, Reda
Abo-Sobehy, Ghada Fathey
Radi, Abd-Elgawad
coredata.dc:description: A nanocomposite of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots (GO@GQDs) were used for the first time to probe the ds-DNA-dimethoate (DMT) interaction and detect the DMT at a trace level. Graphene quantum dots were electrodeposited onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE), followed by casting a graphene oxide layer. This platform was employed to adsorb the ds-DNA to fabricate DNA biosensors (ds-DNA/GO@GQDs/GCE). Microscopic and electrochemical characterizations of the modified electrodes were performed. The GO/GDQs/GCE platform exhibited remarkable electrocatalytic oxidation of DMT from acetate and phosphate buffers. The oxidation process of DMT was pH-dependent and irreversible, proceeding under the mixed adsorption and diffusion-controlled mechanism. The interaction of salmon sperm double-strand DNA (ss-dsDNA) with DMT was investigated using differential pulse voltammetry and UV/vis spectroscopy. Mixed binding modes of intercalation and the electrostatic were ascertained for the interaction of DMT to dsDNA. Such a result was confirmed from the voltammetric data of single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) and the ionic strength study. The UV–Vis study has also supported that finding. The indirect DMT detection is verified by the drop in DNA oxidation signals or increasing DPV peak currents conducted from [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe. The decrease in the DNA peak currents linearly correlated to DMT linear range of 10−15 M to 10−10 M with a limit of detection about 10−15 M. The same detection limit was achieved through the increasing the peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at DMT linear range of 10−15 to 10−12 M. The mechanism of DMT oxidation on GO/GQDs/GCE was proposed and confirmed by forming a self-assembled layer (SAM) of DMT onto the gold electrode (AuE). The surface DMT undergoes anodic oxidation with E p close to the one recorded at GO/GQDs modified surface, referring that the amide group is only possible to oxidize, and the sulfur-containing groups are involved in SAM. The DMT-SAM/AuE would present additional evidence for the surface analysis of DMT-DNA interaction where a change in the DMT peak current upon adding DNA was recorded. ds-DNA-GO@GQDs/GCE sensor exhibits a rapid response, reproducibility, a low detection limit, and high selectivity. The present work may expand the use of GO/GQDs modifiers in the field of electrochemical sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectra of GQDs, inset is the fluorescence spectrum of GQDs (A), SEM images of the GCE modification with GO/GQDs (B), the adsorption of ds-DNA on GO/GQDs/GCE (C), and interaction of ds-DNA sensor with DMT (D).

(A), (B) CVs and EIS spectra of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, 2 mM in PB pH 7 (0.2 M) conducted from GCE, GQDs/GCE, GO/GQDs/GCE, DNA/GO@GQDs/GCE biosensor and after binding to DMT, 10−15 M for 5 s. from acetate buffer pH 5.2.

(A) CVs of 10−3 M DMT oxidation at GCE, GO/GCE, and GO/GQDs/GCE from phosphate buffer pH 3 (0.2 M), inset: DPVs of 10−3 M DMT oxidation at the same electrodes, (B) Effect of phosphate buffer pH values on i p and E p of DMT oxidation, (C) CVs of DMT oxidation at GO/GQDs modified GCE at different scan rate values of 10–600 mV s−1 in PB, pH 3, insets: the square root of scan rate vs. i p and log i p vs. log scan rate. (D) DPVs of DMT oxidation at GO/GQDs/GCE at the concentration range of 5 × 10−6 to 75 × 10−6 M (curves b–g) from phosphate buffer pH 3 (0.2 M), inset is the standard plot of i p vs. CDMT.

(A) UV–visible absorption spectra of 1 × 10−5 M DMT mixed with ds-DNA amounts from 5 to 50 µg/mL in acetate buffer pH 5.2, inset are the UV–visible absorption spectra of 50 µg/mL DNA in the same buffer after mixing with DMT at 10−9 M to 10−4 M. (B) DPVs of 1 × 10−3 M DMT in the presence of 0.0, 0.1, 1.0, 5.0, 50 and 100 µg/mL DNA in acetate buffer (pH 5.2) at GO/GQDS/GCE after incubation 5 s, inset are the effect of DNA concentration the E p and i p of DMT oxidation behavior.

(A) DPVs of 1.0 × 10−3 M DMT and 1.0 µg/mL DNA in the presence of different concentrations of KCl from 0.0 mM to 90.0 mM, in 0.02 M acetate buffer; pH 5.2 at the GO/GQDS/GCE, inset is the KCl (mM) vs. i p of DMT. (B) DPVs of 10−3 M DMT after mixing to ssDNA: 0.1 µg/mL, 5.0 µg/mL, inset is the DPVs of DMT after binding to dsDNA and ssDNA, 1.0 µg/mL. (C) DPVs of 500 µg/mL DNA in 0.02 M acetate buffer pH 5.2 after mixing with different concentrations of DMT from 0.0 M to 0.1 mM at GO/GQDS/GCE, insets are the effect of DMT concentration the E p and i p of guanine oxidation behavior.

(A) DPVs of DNA biosensors constructed at GCE and after modification with GO, GQDs and GO/GQDs by conditioning at the potential of 0.5 V for 300 s, inset is the bar chart of DNA oxidation redox signals vs. the electrode. (B) DPVs of DNA biosensors obtained by physical adsorption of 500 µg/mL for 10 min and accumulation 0.5 V, 300 s on GO/GQDs/GCE, inset (a) effect of adsorption time on the DNA oxidation redox signals, inset (b) influence of ds-DNA concentration on their redox signals from acetate buffer, pH 5.2 on GO/GQDs/GCE surface by adsorption.

(A) DPVs recorded from the DNA biosensor at 3 mg/mL after incubation 5 s. in DMT (10−15 to 10−7 M) in 0.02 M acetate buffer pH 5.2, insets (a) and (b) are the calibration plots based on the guanine and adenine peaks, respectively. (B) DPVs of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− recorded from DNA biosensor at 500 µg/mL after incubation in DMT of different concentrations of 10−15 to 10−11 M, inset is the calibration curve of the (Δi/i 0)% vs. log CDMT. (C) CVs of Self-assembled monolayer of DMT on AuE in acetate buffer at interaction to DNA 1 µg/mL, inset is the DPVs of same electrode modifications. (D) The bar chart of the ds-DNA biosensor selectivity against a mixture of DMT 10−12 M with 10−12 M ALA and CBZ, and 1 mM glucose from acetate buffer (0.02 M) pH 5.2.

head.ce:title: Graphene oxide/graphene quantum dots: A platform for probing ds-DNA-dimethoate interaction and dimethoate sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Damietta University, Damietta 34517, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Damietta University, Damietta 34517, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nanocomposite of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots (GO@GQDs) were used for the first time to probe the ds-DNA-dimethoate (DMT) interaction and detect the DMT at a trace level. Graphene quantum dots were electrodeposited onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE), followed by casting a graphene oxide layer. This platform was employed to adsorb the ds-DNA to fabricate DNA biosensors (ds-DNA/GO@GQDs/GCE). Microscopic and electrochemical characterizations of the modified electrodes were performed. The GO/GDQs/GCE platform exhibited remarkable electrocatalytic oxidation of DMT from acetate and phosphate buffers. The oxidation process of DMT was pH-dependent and irreversible, proceeding under the mixed adsorption and diffusion-controlled mechanism. The interaction of salmon sperm double-strand DNA (ss-dsDNA) with DMT was investigated using differential pulse voltammetry and UV/vis spectroscopy. Mixed binding modes of intercalation and the electrostatic were ascertained for the interaction of DMT to dsDNA. Such a result was confirmed from the voltammetric data of single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) and the ionic strength study. The UV–Vis study has also supported that finding.

The indirect DMT detection is verified by the drop in DNA oxidation signals or increasing DPV peak currents conducted from [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe. The decrease in the DNA peak currents linearly correlated to DMT linear range of 10−15 M to 10−10 M with a limit of detection about 10−15 M. The same detection limit was achieved through the increasing the peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at DMT linear range of 10−15 to 10−12 M.

The mechanism of DMT oxidation on GO/GQDs/GCE was proposed and confirmed by forming a self-assembled layer (SAM) of DMT onto the gold electrode (AuE). The surface DMT undergoes anodic oxidation with E p close to the one recorded at GO/GQDs modified surface, referring that the amide group is only possible to oxidize, and the sulfur-containing groups are involved in SAM. The DMT-SAM/AuE would present additional evidence for the surface analysis of DMT-DNA interaction where a change in the DMT peak current upon adding DNA was recorded. ds-DNA-GO@GQDs/GCE sensor exhibits a rapid response, reproducibility, a low detection limit, and high selectivity. The present work may expand the use of GO/GQDs modifiers in the field of electrochemical sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Deoxyribonucleic acid (ds-DNA) recognition to small molecular weight targets has attracted the researchers' focus since discovering its structure, such as drugs, carcinogens, pesticides, and environmental contaminants. The interfacial recognition of ds-DNA to pesticides is used to probe their toxicity on non-targeted species and develop indirect detection of those molecules.

The change in the DNA and molecules redox signals has indirectly monitored DNA damage or the small molecule on such biosensors. ds-DNA recognizes and binds to small molecules through intercalation, electrostatic, and groove binding [1,2]. UV–Vis spectroscopy [3], fluorescent [4,5], circular dichroism spectroscopy[4], and electroanalytical methods [6–8] have been reported for the probing of ds-DNA-small targets (i.e., primarily drugs and few are pesticides) interactions. Electroanalytical techniques are powerful due to their simplicity and quiet sensitivity. Thus, they have been demonstrated as valuable tools for exploring pesticide–DNA interactions [9–12].

Dimethoate (DMT) is a systemic and contact organophosphorus pesticide against many insects, mites, and houseflies [13]. Because of its low cost and efficiency, DMT is widely used to protect fruit, olive trees, grapes, tobacco, and ornamental from insects [14]. As a neurotoxin, DMT inhibits the acetylcholinesterase activity by its oxon metabolite, dimethoxon. The consequences are cholinergic toxicity, damage to human health, and even fatal effects [15]. Due to its persistence, DMT is carried by rainwater into land, rivers, and lakes [17]. Moreover, dimethoate's residues and metabolites were detected in vegetables [18] and fruits [19]. The ingestion of dimethoate causes more severe lethal poisoning than chlorpyrifos (23.1 and 8%, respectively) [20,21]. According to WHO, the maximum permissible limit of dimethoate in drinking waters was established as 0.006 mg/L [22]. As a result, many countries have set restrictions on the acceptable level of DMT residue in agricultural products [23]. Dimethoate severely affected male mice's reproductive system and sperm [24] and induced DNA damage in Rats [25].

The reported analytical methods for DMT include chromatographic [26,27], colorimetric [28–30], fluorescent [23,31,32], and electrochemical based approaches [33–35]. Some of these methods provide accepted selectivity and sensitivity. However, the complex sample preparation, the time-consuming, and the sophisticated apparatus restrict their popularity. Voltammetric-based approaches have not achieved the detection of DMT at the required sensitivity.

Based on the toxicity effects of DMT on the DNA, this concept could be used to transduce such binding at the ds-DNA interface (i.e., ds-DNA-based biosensor). The electrochemical probing of such interaction presents a promising alternative for indirect DMT assay at a trace level.

In the last decades, efforts were devoted to integrating carbon nanomaterials into the electrochemical transduction of DNA sensing [36,37]. The adaptation of graphene and graphene-based nanomaterials in the electrochemical detection of DNA [38,39] has been demonstrated. To the best of our knowledge, no reports were found on monitoring DMT- DNA interaction at ds-DNA modified graphene oxide (GO)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposite.

GQDs, a relativity new member of graphene nanomaterial of size below 10 nm, are known as zero-dimensional materials, and their properties derive from graphene [40,41]. GQDs have been easily synthesized by the pyrolysis of several precursors such as citric acid [42], glucose [43], and l-glutamic acid [44]. GQDs and their doped derivatives have attracted researchers' interest in developing optical and electrochemical (bio) sensors [45,46]. In particular for the electrochemical biosensors based on single-stranded ssDNA [47–50] and dsDNA for interaction with drugs [39]. Graphene oxide-based composites have shown exciting applications in energy storage devices [51,52] and electrocatalytic-based methods [53,54]. At graphene oxide modified electrodes, the electrochemical detection of ds-DNA interaction with drugs [55,56] and pesticides [57] have been reported. No literature has reported the use of GO/GQDs nanocomposite in ds-DNA-pesticide interaction so far.

Unlike the reported DMT assays, we aim to probe its interaction with dsDNA using the electrochemical approaches at GO/GQDs nanohybrid and employed as an indirect assay for DMT. The change in the DNA and DMT oxidation signals upon the interaction was monitored. The DNA biosensors have been characterized using electroanalytical and surface scanning electron microscopy (SEM) techniques. GO/GQDs electrode exhibited remarkable electrocatalytic oxidation of DMT and dsDNA compared to either glassy carbon electrode or individual modifiers. The mechanism of DMT oxidation from GO-GQDs/GCE was proposed and confirmed. DNA-DMT binding mode was studied using the change in redox signals of DMT and DNA and the UV–vis technique. The ds-DNA concentration and adsorption time significantly affect the DNA peak currents oxidation from GO/GQDs/GCE. At the dsDNA interface, highly sensitive detection of DMT was constructed in combination with a nanocomposite of GO/GQDs.

PGSTAT 302N Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat (Eco-Chemie, The Netherlands) interfaced by Nova 1.11 software was used to conduct the electrochemical measurements of cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). A three-electrode system was used for all measurements; bare and modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (2 mm diameter) with nanomaterials as the working electrode, a Pt wire as the auxiliary electrode, and an Ag|AgCl|KCl (3 M KCl) as a reference electrode were used. For the electrochemical surface characterization experiments, CV and DPV signals were conducted from equimolar of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at sweeping the potential from 0.2 V and + 0.8 V with a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 for CV and a step potential of 5 mV and the amplitude of 20 mV for DPV. The morphology of various electrode modifications was analyzed using the scanning electron microscope(SEM)(JEOLJSM 6510 lv).

In DNA and DMT measurements, DPVs were conducted at a potential range +0.5 V to +1.4 V at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1, a step potential of 5 mV, and an amplitude of 20 mV. The impedance spectra were recorded over the 100 kHz–0.01 Hz frequency range at a bias potential of 0.23 V and amplitude of 10 mV. The impedance data were represented in the complex impedance (Nyquist plot), and the Nova 1.11 software was used to analyze the impedance spectra using an appropriate equivalent electrical circuit. All pH measurements were made on a digital meter (a MettlerToledoMP225, Switzerland) with glass combination electrode, which was previously calibrated with standard buffers. The UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were performed by a PerkinElmer spectrometer (Lambda 850) and fluorescence (LS 55). In most electrochemical measurements, the acetate buffer in the pH range of 3.5 to 5.2 (0.02 M) and the phosphate buffer in the acidic to neutral (0.2 M) are used as the supporting electrolytes.

Salmon sperm DNA sodium salt (Sigma, D-1626, USA), containing 41.2% G-C base pairs with molecular weight 3 × 106 Da (≈2000 base pairs), was used as received [58]. The DNA concentration (in moles of base pairs per liter) was determined spectrophotometrically at 260 nm using a molar extinction coefficient of 6600 L mol−1 cm−1 [59]. The purity of DNA was estimated by the ratio of optical density at 260 nm to that at 280 nm (A260/A280), which was in the range 1.8–1.9, indicating that the DNA was sufficiently free from protein [60]. Stock solutions of dsDNA (5 mg/mL) were prepared with Tris–EDTA buffer solution (10 mM Tris–HCl, 1 mM EDTA, TE, pH 8.0) and kept frozen. More diluted solutions of dsDNA were prepared with 0.02 M acetate buffer, pH 5.2. Dimethoate (Nasr thoate 40% EC) was obtained from a local distributor. All other reagents are supplied at good purity, and solutions were prepared using double distilled water. A stock solution of dimethoate (10−2 M) was prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of the insecticide in methanol and storing it in the dark at 4 °C. New working solutions from dimethoate were made by diluting the stock solution with the appropriate buffer. An equimolar of K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (2 mM) was prepared in phosphate buffer pH 7.2 (0.2 M). Acetate buffer was prepared by mixing the required acetic acid and sodium acetate; the pH was adjusted to desired values with 1 M NaOH solution. The phosphate buffer solutions were obtained using phosphoric acid, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, and potassium monophosphate. Denatured single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) was produced by heating the dsDNA solution in a water bath at 97 °C for 5 min, immediately followed by rapid cooling in an ice bath.

Graphene oxide was prepared according to the modified hummer method [61], and GQDs were synthesized using the bottom-up pyrolysis method, as reported elsewhere [42]. Briefly, 2 g citric acid was placed in a 100 mL round bottom flask and then heated to 200 ℃ in an oil bath. About 30 min later, the color of the liquid changed from colorless to orange, which implied the formation of GQDs. After a dropwise addition of 1 M NaOH solution and vigorous stirring until the pH of the GQDs solution up to 8.0 is obtained.

Before the modification, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished using ultrafine emery paper, 0.3 mm, 0.05 mm alumina slurry in sequence, and sonicated successively in water for 2 min. The electrochemical cleaning was performed from 0.1 M H2SO4 by 10 CV scans from −1.0 to +1.0 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. After rinsing thoroughly with water, GCE was subjected to the GQDs modification; according to published reports [62], with a slight change, the GQDs solution pH 8.0 was electrodeposited on the cleaned GCE surface by potential sweeping between −1.5 and +2.5 V with 100 mV s−1 for 20 cycles (the optimal condition for fabricating the GQDs/GCE). Consequently, the 10 µL of GO (10 mg/ml) was cast on the GQDs/GCE until dry. To pick up the best platform for ds-DNA oxidation, different electrode modifiers of GO/GQDs, GQDs, GO as well as the bare GCE were used to immobilize ds-DNA (0.5 mg/mL) from acetate buffer pH 5.2 (0.02 M) by applying a constant potential of +0.3 V for 500 sec. At GO@GQDs/GCE platform, the covalent attachment of dsDNA exhibited the highest oxidation peak currents to the other modifies. At this end, the physical adsorption of the same ds-DNA amount at 10 mins was compared to electrochemical methods and found to be best. Various time intervals from 10 min to 60 mins were used for DNA adsorption. The physical adsorption at 30 mins was the preferable approach to record the best oxidation signals from the ds-DNA modified biosensor. The concentration of dsDNA was also tested to the optimal conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probing of dimethoate-DNA interaction in solution
ce:section.ce:para: The voltammetric behavior of DMT was investigated on GCE bare and following the modification with GO, GQDs, and GO/GQDs. The CV and DPV signals were conducted from 10−3 M DMT in phosphate buffer pH 3 (0.2 M). Phosphate buffer was chosen to investigate the DMT catalytic oxidation, while the acetate buffer of pH 5.2 was the best electrolyte for dsDNA-DMT interaction. Two titration experiments were performed to investigate the binding of ss-dsDNA to DMT in solution: keeping the fixed concentration of ds-DNA or DMT and conducting the voltammetric and UV-absorption measurements in acetate buffer solutions (0.02 M, pH 5.2) upon changing the counterpart solutions of DMT or ds-DNA. The influence of ionic strength on the complex formation between DMT and DNA has been studied in the presence of different concentrations of KCl (30, 60, and 90 mM) in acetate buffer solution (0.02 M, pH 5.2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The surface probing of DNA-Dimethoate interaction
ce:section.ce:para: The recognition of DMT to the dsDNA biosensor was employed to confirm DNA-DMT interaction and detect DMT indirectly via measuring the change in the ds-DNA oxidation peaks of guanine and adenine. The dsDNA/GO@GQDs/GCE biosensor was incubated in various concentrations of DMT for 5 s in 0.02 M acetate buffer pH 5.2; after rinsing with acetate buffer, DPVs of DNA was recorded from the same buffer.

The extent of dsDNA binding to DMT was estimated by recording the change of the oxidation peak currents for DNA nucleobases (i.e., guanine and adenine). Moreover, the increase in the peak current of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe was used to confirm that binding. We found that the anodic peak currents of both guanine and adenine decreased in a short time of 5 s. at a trace amount of DMT. So, the interaction time could not be changed even at a trace concentration of DNA or DMT. Thus, the interaction time was kept at 5 s.

The as-prepared GQDs were characterized by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopic studies. A dispersed sample of the GQDs displayed an absorption band at 340 nm (Fig. 1 A) due to the n–π* transition of CO bonds [63]. No absorption band was observed in the spectrum of citric acid (Fig. 1A, dot curve), which confirm the formation of GQDs. The fluorescence spectrum of the GQDs (Fig. 1A, inset) shows an emission band at 448 nm with a small shoulder at 484 nm when excited at 365 nm. These bands were observed due to a bandgap associated with quantum confinement of GQDs, in contrast to graphene of infinitesimal bandgap, limiting its luminescent properties [64]. These results agreed with reports in the literature [63,64].

SEM image of GO modified GQDs/GCE modified electrodes is presented. At the surface of GO/GQDs (Fig. 1B), few layers of GO were crumpled and folded into a typical wrinkled structure with irregular edges [38]. A different view is observed after the adsorption of ds-DNA onto the GO modified surface (Fig. 1C) with more prominent transparent wrinkles with more dense layers, which looks like folded paper. An insignificant difference between DNA/GO/GQDs surface and after the DMT binding is observed, except for the density of wrinkles and sheet folding (Fig. 1D). At the binding of DMT to DNA modified surface, the folding of the sheet and the thickness of the crumple layer become low.

Cyclic voltammograms and the impedance spectra were recorded from 2 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1: 1) solution in PB pH 7 to monitor the fabrication process of GO@GQDs/GCE platform and the DNA biosensor. As depicted from (Fig. 2 A, red curve), after the electrodeposition of GQDs on GCE, the CV recorded a remarkable increase in peak current (79.42 µA) from [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe to the bare GCE (53.42 µA), indicates the high conductivity of GQDs to GCE. A small radius semi-circle with a Ret of 270 Ω (Fig. 2B, red curve) was supported such CV behavior, referring to the fast electron transfer rate of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−.

On the other hand, at the casting of GO on GQDs/GCE and the physical adsorption of ds-DNA (Fig. 2A, green and blue curves), a significant drop in the peak currents of (26.97 µA and 32.58 µA) and peak separation values of ΔE p 0.085 V and 0.1107 V, respectively. EIS data as Nyquist plots were consistent with CV results. The enlarged radii of semi-circles of Nyquist plots (Ret = 391 Ω, 1.2 KΩ) were also measured following the modification with GO and DNA adsorption (Fig. 2B, green and blue curves). Such behavior is expected due to the lower conductivity of GO and the negatively charged phosphate backbone of the ds-DNA that repels the negatively charged redox marker to reach the surface. Upon immersing in DMT 10−15 M for 5 s, the peak current increased to 34.22 µA, and the EIS value decreased to about Ret (914 Ω). Such values indicate the interaction of DMT to the DNA on the surface, where the exposed negatively charged phosphate backbones become low.

The effective surface area of the various electrode modifications was calculated by conducting cyclic voltammograms of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at a scan rate range of 20 to 750 mV s−1. According to the Randles–Ševčík relation of

, Where i p is the anodic peak current, D is the diffusion coefficient of K3Fe(CN)6 in solution (7.6 × 10−5 cm2 s−1), n is the number of electrons transferred in the redox reaction, ѵ is the potential scan rate, (V s−1). C is the bulk concentration of K3Fe(CN)6 (M). The linear dependence of i p on the square root of scan rate (ν½) was achieved. The electroactive area was calculated from the slope and found to be 2.51 cm2, 6.03 cm2, and 2.3 cm2 for bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, and GO/GQDs/GCE, respectively. The catalytic activity of GO/GQDs/GCE and GQDs/GCE would be correlated to their high surface area and their electrical conductivity.

So far, only two publications have detected dimethoate as an electroactive molecule [34,35]. The voltammetric behavior of DMT (10−3 M) was investigated in phosphate buffer solution pH 3 (0.2 M) over a GCE and after the modification with GO, GQDs, and GO/GQDs using CV and DPV. As observed from Fig. 3 A, the bare GCE electrode did not show any peak, indicating a slow electron transfer rate and the stability of DMT over the studied potential range. At the GCE modified with GO, GQDs, and GO/GQDs, well-defined oxidation peaks were observed (Fig. 3A), pronounced at GO/GQDs/GCE, at a potential of 1.089 V and peak current of 2.47 µA.

The appearance of such a peak indicating electrocatalytic action of these nanomodifications for the electrochemical oxidation of DMT. Such anodic peaks may be corresponding to direct oxidation of the amide moiety of DMT to an alcohol group. Since no cathodic peaks were observed during the reverse CV scan (Fig. 3A), we conclude that the DMT electrooxidation process is irreversible. The DPVs of anodic oxidation of DMT are also recorded to support the CVs (Fig. 3A, inset). A significant negative shift in the DPV peak potential about 135.9 mV to 141 mV was observed at GO/GQDs/GCE to GO/GCE and GQDs/GCE, respectively (Fig. 3A, inset curves b to d). The shift in the potential indicates the catalytic effect of the GO/GQDs towards the DMT oxidation to the individual GO or GQDs. Combining GQDs and GO mainly facilitates the electron transfer between DMT and electrode and thus improves the electrode's catalytic activity.

Acetate and phosphate buffer have been tested for the best oxidation of DMT at pH 5; a well-defined oxidation peak was recorded from the phosphate buffer. Thus, it is used to conduct the mechanism of DMT oxidation. As the best oxidation signal was obtained at GO@GQDs/GCE, this nanohybrid was used to record the DP voltammograms of DMT 10−3 M from phosphate buffer at a pH range from 2 to 7 (0.2 M) (Fig. 3B). There is a significant change in the i p at various pH values, referring to the strong dependency of the DMT oxidation on the pH of the supporting electrolyte. It is noteworthy that the influence of pH is insignificant on the DMT peak potential (Fig. 3B, line b), indicating that its oxidation process does not involve proton transfer. The peak current is enhanced by raising the pH value from 2 to 3, while it decreases at a higher pH value, indicating the best working solution pH of 3 (Fig. 3B, line a).

The effect of the scan rate on the DMT oxidation was also examined to clarify the electrocatalytic mechanism from GO@GQDs/GCE (Fig. 3C). A positive shift in the peak potential (E p) was observed with an increasing sweep rate of 10 to 600 mV s−1, indicating the irreversible nature of the oxidation process. The results presented in (Fig. 3C, inset a) revealed a linear increase of I p of DMT with the square root of the scan rates (v1/2) as well as the shift of peak potentials to more positive values. A linear relationship between I p and v1/2 is observed i p = 0.155 υ½ −0.51 with (R2 = 0.981), demonstrating that the electrode process is controlled by diffusion. On the other hand, the linearity dependence of log i p on the log v with slope values of 0.708 was obtained (Fig. 3C, inset b) Log i p = 0.708 log υ −1.424 (R = 0.9728). With this value, the oxidation process of DMT on GO/GQDs/GCE was not controlled by a pure diffusion (the value of 0.5 for pure diffusion). It has undergone oxidation under a mixed diffusion and adsorption process.

The oxidation mechanism of DMT may proceed on the oxidation of the amido group, the only possible group to get oxidation. The other sulfur-containing groups, as reported they were only reduced on the Hg electrode. A self-assemble monolayer (SAM) of dimethoate was formed on the Au electrode (AuE) to confirm such an oxidation pathway, followed by testing its redox behavior. Such SAM of DMT has also been proposed to evidence the interaction of surface DMT to DNA (see the results in the section of surface probing of DNA-DMT interaction). At the electrode surface, the amido moiety (OC-NH) is free to oxidize from the Au electrode, while the phosphorothioate group forms a self-assembled monolayer. At DMT-SAM/Au, two defined oxidation peaks were recorded at 0.83 V and 1.08 V, assigned to the Au oxidation and DMT. Upon binding to the ds-DNA solution, the peak of DMT disappeared while the AuE peak was retained, confirming the surface DMT and its interaction with DNA.

Since the oxidation of DMT is not a pure diffusion-controlled mechanism, there is some contribution to the surface process. Thus, the accumulation time was optimized for highly sensitive oxidation DMT signals. As a result, the optimal accumulation time was found to be 40 mins. Therefore, series of concentrations of DMT was used to build up the calibration curve, where for each concentration, the anodic peak current is recorded in a separate electrode surface. As seen in Fig. 3D, linearly was achieved in a wide range of 5–75 µM in the equation: i p (µA) = 0.1034 + 0.0021 CDMT (μM) with (R2 = 0.989). The actual limit of detection was estimated to be 5 µM. This LOD value is comparable to other reported methods [34,35]. It should be noted that no reports are available on the electrochemical determination of DMT at GO@GQDs/GCE.

The binding of ds-DNA with DMT was investigated by measuring the UV-absorption spectra of dimethoate and ds-DNA in the absence and presence of different concentrations of another counterpart. Dimethoate has a sharp absorption peak at 211 nm and a broad absorption peak at 263 nm (Fig. 4 A). Upon mixing with dsDNA, insignificant changes in the peak positions were observed from DMT or dsDNA.

The absorption band at 211 nm, very close to the DNA peak, showed an increase in the peak intensity (hyperchromic effect). In contrast, the broad absorption band of DMT or DNA at 263 nm increased significantly with successive additions of dsDNA (it could be the peak of the DNA itself). The hyperchromic effect may be due to the intercalation of the DMT between the dsDNA base pairs. The intercalation between the DMT and dsDNA was confirmed by observing the absorption spectra of DMT at various concentrations of ds-DNA (5 µg/mL to 50 µg/mL) (Fig. 4A). The opposite case was investigated as well, where the absorption spectra of ds-DNA were observed at a fixed amount (50 µg/mL) upon mixing to DMT of different amounts (10−9 to 10−4 M) (Fig. 4A, inset). Again, the hyperchromic effect was pronounced in the band of 212 nm with an increasing concentration of DMT in solution (Fig. 4A, inset). The increase of absorption maxima (hyperchromic effect) of DMT or dsDNA binding molecules is typical of ds-DNA intercalation [65]. As the absorption spectra of DMT and DNA are very close, their interaction mechanism needs to confirm. Thus, the redox behavior of dimethoate, as well as ds-DNA, will be analyzed to ascertain such interaction.

To our knowledge, no reports on the DNA-DMT interaction based on GO/GQDs nanocomposite were developed so far. The DPVs of 1 × 10−3 M DMT in the absence and presence of ds-DNA (0.1 µg/mL to 100 µg/mL) at GO@GQDs/GCE in acetate buffer solution (0.02 M, pH 5.2) are presented in (Fig. 4B).

The voltammogram of DMT without DNA featured a well-defined anodic peak at 1.1597 V (Fig. 4B, curve a) with a peak current of 2.447 µA, whereas, by adding 0.1 µg/mL DNA to DMT solution at equilibrium time 5 s., a significant drop in the i p of DMT of (0.549 µA, i.e., 78.5%) was recorded with no applicable shift in the E p. On mixing to 1 µg/mL DNA, three well-separated peaks were observed at 0.787 V, 1.049 V, and 1.195 V with ip values of (0.063 µA, 0.187 µA, and 0.517 µA). These peaks were assigned to the guanine, adenine, and the DMT oxidation after positive shift, respectively. The positive shift of about 35.3 mV may be attributed to the interaction of DMT with DNA in an intercalative mode.

This change in the oxidation behavior of DMT (i.e., drop in i p and positive shift in Ep) indicates the interaction of DMT and ds-DNA. More importantly, upon increasing the DNA concentration, the extent of interaction increased as explored by the recording significant positive shift in the potential and gradual drop in the peak current and leveling off at DNA, 500 µg/mL (Fig. 4B, inset). This revealed the interaction between DNA and DMT. It is noteworthy that the oxidation signals of DNA bases were also recorded even at low concentrations, which are well separated from the DMT peak. The drop in peak current may be caused by electrochemically inactive DMT–DNA adduct [66]. The positive shift of oxidation peak potential confirms the intercalative interaction between DMT and DNA [67]. The decrease of the DMT's peak current after adding DNA in excess is derived from the intercalation of the bulky dsDNA to DMT, which renders the diffusion of DMT to the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ionic strength on DMT-DNA interaction
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of ionic strength on the interaction of DMT to dsDNA was also studied. At a high ionic strength solution, the charge compensation of the nucleotide phosphates caused by cations would hinder the electrostatic binding effect. Thus, the binding based on an intercalation mechanism should be virtually unaffected [68], while the one based on electrostatic binding will be decreased.

In acetate buffer pH 5.2, the DPV response of 1 × 10−3 M DMT containing 1 µg/mL DNA doubled from 0.32 µA to 0.68 µA after the addition of 30 mM KCl (Fig. 5 A), indicating that DMT–DNA adduct was sensitive to the ionic strength in pH 5.2 buffer solution. This finding was confirmed the electrostatic interaction between DMT and DNA, besides the intercalative interaction. It is noteworthy that by adding KCl solution to increase the ionic strength of ions, DMT undergoes oxidation at a high potential value to the solution free from KCl, i.e., a positive shift of 15 mV, 10 mV, 14.5 mV for 30, 60, and 90 mM KCl, respectively (Fig. 5A). At higher KCl such as 60 mM and 90 mM, the peak current of DMT oxidation increased, but the remarkable enhancement was at 30 mM KCl (Fig. 5A, inset). More importantly, the adenine redox behavior was affected much, where a negative shift in potential and increase in the current were observed. Besides, the guanine oxidation signal was not recorded. The result confirms that electrostatic interaction mode contributes to the intercalative one in the binding of DMT to ds-DNA under such a solution condition.
By adding KCl, the interaction of phosphate backbones of DNA and K+ occurs, releasing some bounded DMT from the DMT–DNA complex. Thus, an enhanced oxidation peak current of DMT is observed.

DPVs of DMT were recorded in the presence of denatured single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) and compared to the ds-DNA case (Fig. 5B, inset). It is known that ds-DNA interact with small molecules via its negatively charged phosphates through the electrostatic interactions as well as the intercalative binding, where the interior part of the DNA double helix intercalates the small molecule. If the interaction mode is intercalative, its strength will decrease in the presence of ssDNA. However, the electrostatic interactions may continue to operate even after DNA denaturation.

In the absence of DMT, the ssDNA exhibited a well-defined guanine oxidation peak compared to the adenine one (Fig. 5B). A remarkable decrease in DMT oxidation peak was measured by adding DMT to the ssDNA solutions (0.5 µg/mL and 1.0 µg/mL). These results implied that there is an interaction between DMT and ssDNA. The strength of binding of DMT to ds-DNA was compared with ssDNA (Fig. 5B inset, curves b, and c). By adding ssDNA to the DMT solution, a significant drop in the DPV of DMT (Fig. 5B inset, curve c) was observed compared with dsDNA (Fig. 5B, inset, curve b). These results indicated that the electrostatic binding mode also contributed to DNA-DMT interaction [69,70]. Based on the ssDNA and the ionic strength studies, the DMT-dsDNA interaction is an electrostatic-based mode. However, the voltammetric data confirmed the intercalation one; thus, the binding method of dsDNA to DMT is not pure. It is a compromise between the electrostatic and intercalative.

For the titration of a fixed dose of ds-DNA and varying the dimethoate, DP voltammograms of 500 µg/mL dsDNA and dimethoate of concentration from 10−9 to 10−4 M in 0.02 M acetate buffer (pH 5.2), after interaction time of 5 s. are presented (Fig. 5C). At zero adding of DMT, DNA oxidation peaks of guanine and adenine (1.957 µA and 1.853 µA) at peak potential of (0.736 V and 1.038 V) were observed (Fig. 5C, curve a). A significant drop in the DNA oxidation peak currents was obtained at the binding to DMT 10−9 M to 10−4 M (Fig. 5C, curves b to h). The drop in the DNA peak currents evaluated the extent of DNA-DMT binding, which was 41.46% to 76.68% at the interaction to DMT doses 10−9 to 10−4 M for guanine and 18.79% to 65.41% for adenine.

A positive shift in the guanine peak potential was observed at a high concentration of DMT (Fig. 5C, inset, red line). A negatively charged redox marker is used to demonstrate that the decrease in DNA oxidation signals derived from its interaction with DMT is not due to the viscosity effect or the blocking electrode surface. Thus, the CV of GO@GQDs/GCE was conducted from a K3[Fe (CN)6] in acetate buffer, pH 5, then compared to a mixture of 2 mM K3[Fe (CN)6] and dsDNA (500 µg/mL) and after further mixing the redox marker, dsDNA, and DMT in acetate buffer, pH 5 (data not shown). No significant changes were observed in the peak of the redox marker without and in the presence of dsDNA. On the other hand, a remarkable drop in the peak current was recorded upon adding DMT, indicating DNA interaction to DMT.

The ds-DNA biosensors constructed at various electrode modifications by conditioning in +0.3 V at 500 s from 500 µg/mL DNA were tested by recording the DPVs of ds-DNA nucleobases oxidation peaks in acetate buffer pH 5 (0.2 M). As can be seen in Fig. 6 A, well-defined voltammetric shapes and intense responses for ds-DNA were obtained from GO/GQDs/GCE platform (Fig. 6A, curve a): the oxidation peak currents and their potential peak values of guanine (i p = 1.57 µA, E p = 0.73 V), and adenine (i p = 1.79 µA, E p = 1.03 V), respectively.

The guanine oxidation peak was used to compare the performance of different electrode modifications. About 24.29% more intense i p value was recorded from the GO/GQDs/GCE electrode (with a positive shift of about 76.6 mV from GCE). Hence, the GO/GQDs/GCE electrode was chosen for all subsequent measurements. At the GO@GQDs/GCE platform, the method and immobilization time, in addition to the DNA concentration, were optimized. As observed from (Fig. 6B, curve a), the anodic peaks of DNA bases recorded from the DNA biosensor formed by physical adsorption of DNA for 10 mins are more significant than those measured from the conditioning +0.3 V for 500 s. at the GO@GQDs/GCE.

At this step, the DNA adsorption time was optimized as shown in (Fig. 6B, inset of (a)), the DPV signals of DNA biosensor obtained at adsorption time of 30 mins was the largest to the other biosensors constructed as 5, 10, 45 and 60 min. Finally, the amount of DNA (0.75 to 3 mg/mL) adsorbed was also optimized, the DNA concentration of 2 mg/mL was recorded as the best signal (Fig. 5B, inset of (b)).

At the optimal parameters of the DNA biosensor, it was incubated in DMT solutions of the concentration range of 10−15 to 10−7 M in 0.02 M acetate buffer (pH 5.2) for 5 s; the DPVs were collected (Fig. 7 A). The drop in the DNA adenine peak current was used as a sensor response.

Two pronounced oxidation peaks corresponding to guanine and adenine at the DNA biosensor at 0.72 V and 1.05 V with peak currents of 5.87 µA and 4.39 µA, respectively (Fig. 7A, black curve). DPVs from the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 probe were also used to characterize the DNA biosensor and confirm its recognition to DMT (Fig. 7B). The DNA voltammetric signals were evidenced by the decrease in DPV (Fig. 7B, black curve), because the electronegative phosphate skeletons of DNA prevent [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− from reaching the electrode surface. Upon immersing in DMT of different concentrations for only 5 s, a remarkable drop in guanine/adenine oxidation peaks was observed (Fig. 7A).

The same behavior of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− DPV peaks were conducted, as the increase in the i p values was measured (Fig. 7B); this possibly occurred because of the interaction of DMT or its electrochemical oxidation product to ds-DNA increase of the DNA charge transfer. Such interaction may result in a more positive environment on the dsDNA surface (i.e., shielding some of the negatively charged phosphate skeletons) which makes it access the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− anion and record a high signal.

Based on the DMT-dsDNA interaction in solution, the electrostatic and intercalative binding modes govern its binding to DNA. Thus, the electrostatic of DNA to DMT would decrease the negatively charged phosphate backbone exposed to the electrode surface, thus enhancing the peak current of the probe. Simultaneously, the intercalation of DMT within the ds-DNA bases blocks its redox oxidation, thus decline their peak currents.

As observed from Fig. 7A, after only 5 s, the interaction of DMT with the DNA biosensor resulted in a significant drop in the oxidation peaks of adenine and guanine. As the DMT interacted with the dsDNA biosensor at trace amounts, dimethoate's oxidation peak may not appear. The decline of the DPV peak currents of adenine vs. the concentration of DMT was linear in the range of 10−15 to 10−10 M with the equation: (Δi/i 0)% = 109.033 + 0.918 log CDMT (M) (R2 = 0.987) (Fig. 7A, inset b).

Similarly, the increase in the DPVs peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− from DNA biosensors was measured with DMT at various concentrations from 10−15 to 10−11 M with a linear equation: (Δi/i 0)% = 103.56 + 5.62 log CDMT (M) (R2 = 0.978) (Fig. 7B, inset). For both calibration curves, the limit of detection was about 10−15 M DMT.

Compared with some published reports on DMT determination [29,31,33,71], the sensitivity of this method is very high. Furthermore, it is not easy to find publications on modified electrodes where DNA sensor has been used to probe the DNA interaction and indirectly detect the DMT simultaneously.

The interaction of ds-DNA to DMT could be investigated in the opposite process where DMT is immobilized on a gold electrode (AuE) through a self-assembly monolayer of AuE and sulfur-containing groups of DMT. The change in DMT redox signal upon adding DNA introduces additional evidence for DMT interaction to DNA and confirms the DMT oxidation pathway. As depicted from (Fig. 7C), the CV of bare AuE in acetate buffer pH 5 (0.02 M) was observed at E p of 0.83 V and i p of 8.53 µA. In contrast, a CV was measured at the SAM-dimethoate with an additional well-defined peak at E p of 1.08 V and i p of 2.66 µA (Fig. 7C, curve b). This indicating the successful immobilization of dimethoate. On the other hand, on incubating in DNA (1 µg/mL) for 10 s, a negative shift in the Au oxidation peak was observed to 0.816 V and i p of 2.4 µA (Fig. 7C, curve c), in addition to the disappearance of the DMT oxidation peak. These results evidenced the oxidation mechanism of the DMT via its amido moiety and supported its interaction with ds-DNA.

Four ss-dsDNA-based biosensors were recorded the DNA oxidation signals from acetate buffer pH 5.2 (0.02 M). The average relative standard deviation (% RSD) of the peak currents of guanine and adenine was 5.91% and 6.76%, respectively, indicating the acceptable reproducibility of the DNA biosensors.

The specificity of the DNA/GO/GQDs/GCE biosensor was evaluated by measuring its DPV peak currents after binding to DMT, and a mixture of DMT with some pesticides used simultaneously with DMT. The ss-dsDNA biosensors were immersed in a mix of 10−12 M of DMT individually with carbendazim (CBZ) and alachlor (ALA) in addition to mixing with 1.0 mM glucose and left for 5 s before the DPVs of DNA from acetate buffer pH 5.2 were measured. According to the obtained results, the existence of different interfering molecules had a negligible effect on DNA-DMT interaction (Fig. 7D). The change in DNA sensor responses was about 7% and 8% for guanine and adenine in the presence of these interferents. The DNA-GO/GQDs/GCE sensor has an excellent anti-interference capability for selective detection of DMT.

In summary, GO/GQDs nanocomposite was employed to probe the ds-DNA-DMT interaction in solution and at the interface of DNA biosensor for the first time. At the DNA modified GO/GQDs, the indirect detection of DMT was established. The electrocatalytic oxidation of DMT was achieved at GO/GQDs modified electrode in an irreversible and pH-dependent electrode process from phosphate buffer in the acidic range. Direct DMT determination was established on the GO/GQDs/GCE with a linear range of 5 × 10−6 M to 75 × 10−6 M and LOD of ca 5 × 10−6 M. The electroanalytical performance of the GO/GQDs/GCE electrode for the detection of DMT was monitored via its binding to dsDNA. Based on the DNA-DMT interaction in solution, the ssDNA, and ionic strength, the intercalative and electrostatic modes worked cooperatively in binding DMT to DNA at the experiential conditions. The SAM of DMT of AuE evidenced the DMT oxidation pathway and its binding to ds-DNA interaction.

In comparison to the direct detection of DMT based on its oxidation behavior, the DNA biosensor was ultrasensitive. It achieved a wide linear range of 10−15 to 10−10 M with a LOD of 10−15 M. Accordingly, DNA/GO@GQDs/GCE was utilized as a DMT sensor, providing a proper analytical tool study of DMT pollution control. The DNA biosensors exhibited good reproducibility, stability, and selectivity against some common cross reactants. Integrating GO@GQDs nanoplatform with voltammetric techniques presented a promised assay for DMT and probed its interaction to ds-DNA.

Reda Elshafey: Visualization, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Ghada Fathey Abo-Sobehy: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Abd-Elgawad Radi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Supervision.
